```{r}
# The scale (* 100) is crucial! GARCH optimizers fail if the numbers are too tiny (e.g. 0.0001)
res_var <- resid(var_restricted_aic)*100

# Convert the residuals back into a Time Series (xts) object for plotting later
# We use the dates from diff_data, but remember the VAR loses the first 'p' rows
# So we align the dates by tailing the diff_data
# Assicurati che la colonna date sia nel formato corretto
dates_vector <- as.Date(train_var$Data)

# Crea l'oggetto xts usando le ultime 'n' date corrispondenti ai residui
var_residuals_xts <- xts(res_var, 
                         order.by = tail(dates_vector, nrow(res_var)))
colnames(var_residuals_xts) <- c("Spread_Shock", "EURUSD_Shock","EURIBOR3_Shock")

# 2. Specify the Univariate GARCH Models
# Because the VAR already handled the mean, we set the ARMA order to (0,0) with NO mean!
uspec <- ugarchspec(
  mean.model = list(armaOrder = c(0, 0), include.mean = FALSE),
  variance.model = list(garchOrder = c(1, 1), model = "sGARCH"),
  distribution.model = "norm" # You can change to "std" (Student-t) if it fails to converge
)

# Replicate this specification for all of our variables
mspec <- multispec(replicate(3, uspec))

# 3. Specify the Multivariate DCC-GARCH model
# We set VAR = FALSE because we ALREADY did the VAR manually and are feeding it the residuals!
dcc_spec <- dccspec(
  uspec = mspec, 
  VAR = FALSE,
  dccOrder = c(1, 1), 
  distribution = "mvnorm"
)

# 4. Fit the DCC-GARCH model!
# We feed it our scaled VAR residuals
cat("\nFitting DCC-GARCH...\n")
fit_dcc <- dccfit(dcc_spec, data = var_residuals_xts)

# Print the final estimated model
show(fit_dcc)

# Dynamic conditional correlations over time
dcc_corr <- rcor(fit_dcc)        # array: 2x2xT
rho_t    <- dcc_corr[1,2,]       # time series of rho(BTP-Bund, VSTOXX)
plot(rho_t, type = "l", main = "Dynamic Conditional Correlation")

# Conditional volatilities
vol <- sigma(fit_dcc)

# DCC parameters (alpha + beta, should sum < 1)
coef(fit_dcc)

```
```{r}
library(xts)

rho_t2 <- rcor(fit_dcc)[1, 2, ]
resids <- residuals(var_restricted_aic)

plot(zoo(rho_t2, order.by = index(resids)), type = "l",
     main = "Dynamic Conditional Correlation: BTP-Bund vs VSTOXX",
     ylab = "Correlation", xlab = "")
abline(h = mean(rho_t2), col = "red", lty = 2)
```

## Model Diagnostics
```{r}
# 1. Model Diagnostics 
# Standardized residuals should have no ARCH effects remaining
infocriteria(fit_dcc)   # AIC, BIC
plot(fit_dcc, which=2)           # rmgarch gives several diagnostic plots
plot(fit_dcc, which=3)

#colnames(var_fit$y)

# 2. VAR Impulse Response Functions (IRFs)
# This answers "how does the BTP-Bund spread respond to a VSTOXX shock?"
library(vars)
irf_result <- irf(var_restricted_aic, impulse = "d_EURUSD",
                  response = c("d_Spread", "d_EURIBOR3"),
                  n.ahead = 20, boot = TRUE)
plot(irf_result)

irf_result <- irf(var_restricted_aic, impulse = "d_EURIBOR3",
                  response = c("d_Spread", "d_EURUSD"),
                  n.ahead = 20, boot = TRUE)
plot(irf_result)

# 3. DCC-GARCH Volatility Forecast
# A short-horizon forecast of conditional variances and correlations
dcc_forecast <- dccforecast(fit_dcc, n.ahead = 20)
plot(dcc_forecast, which = 3)

# Extract forecasted correlation
rcor(dcc_forecast)

# 4. Variance Decomposition (FEVD)
# Tells you how much of the BTP-Bund spread variance is explained by VSTOXX shocks

fevd_result <- fevd(var_restricted_aic, n.ahead = 20)
plot(fevd_result)
```

## Forecast
```{r}

# Re-fit with out.sample to enable rolling forecast
dcc_fit2 <- dccfit(dcc_spec, data = resids,
                   out.sample = 100)   # hold out last 100 obs

# 1. Unconditional n-ahead forecast
dcc_fore <- dccforecast(dcc_fit2, n.ahead = 20)

# Forecasted correlation
rcor(dcc_fore)   # 2x2xT array

# Forecasted volatilities
sigma(dcc_fore)

# 2. Rolling 1-step-ahead forecast (more meaningful for evaluation)
dcc_roll <- dccforecast(dcc_fit2, n.ahead = 1, n.roll = 99)
rcor(dcc_roll)

# 1-Step Ahead Forecast with VAR
library(vars)

# Forecast 1 step ahead (next trading day)
var_forecast <- predict(var_restricted_aic, n.ahead = 1, ci = 0.95)

# Print the forecast
print(var_forecast)
last_spread <- tail(spread, 1)
forecast_level <- last_spread + var_forecast$fcst$d_Spread[1, "fcst"]
forecast_level

```
## Test the model
We now use the test portion of our data, to see the accuracy of our training model

```{r}

library(vars)
library(xts)
library(ggplot2)

# =========================
# Test set: rolling 1-step-ahead out-of-sample forecast
# =========================

train_multi <- train_var[,-1]
test_multi  <- test_var[,-1]
sum(is.na(train_multi))
# 1. Selezione lag ottimale sul train
lag_sel <- VARselect(train_multi, lag.max = 10, type = "const")
p_opt <- as.numeric(lag_sel$selection["AIC(n)"])

cat("Optimal lag selected on train:", p_opt, "\n")

# 2. Rolling 1-step-ahead forecast sulla serie differenziata "spread"
n_test <- nrow(test_multi)

fc_dspread <- numeric(n_test)
real_dspread <- as.numeric(test_multi[, "d_Spread"])

# Among the candidate rolling window sizes, 400 observations produced the lowest out-of-sample forecast error and  # the most plausible final spread estimate, therefore it was selected as the preferred specification.
window_size <- 400


for (i in 1:n_test) {

  if (i == 1) {
    available_data <- train_multi
  } else {
    available_data <- rbind(train_multi, test_multi[1:(i - 1), ])
  }

  if (nrow(available_data) > window_size) {
    current_train <- available_data[(nrow(available_data) - window_size + 1):nrow(available_data), ]
  } else {
    current_train <- available_data
  }

  var_roll <- VAR(current_train, p = p_opt, type = "const")
  pred_1 <- predict(var_roll, n.ahead = 1, ci = 0.95)

  fc_dspread[i] <- as.numeric(pred_1$fcst$d_Spread[1, "fcst"])
}


# 3. Date del test
test_dates <-test_var[,1]

# 4. Ricostruzione dello spread in livelli
# ultimo valore osservato in livelli prima dell'inizio del test
test_start_date <- test_dates[1]
pos_test_start <- which(index(spread) == test_start_date)
last_level_before_test <- as.numeric(spread[pos_test_start - 1])

# forecast in livelli
fc_level <- last_level_before_test + cumsum(fc_dspread)

# valori reali in livelli
real_level <- as.numeric(spread[test_dates])

# 5. Data frame finale
df_roll <- data.frame(
  Date = as.Date(test_dates),
  RealSpread = real_level,
  ForecastSpread = fc_level,
  RealDiff = real_dspread,
  ForecastDiff = fc_dspread
)

# 6. Grafico livelli
ggplot(df_roll, aes(x = Date)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = RealSpread, color = "Real spread"), linewidth = 1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = ForecastSpread, color = "Rolling forecast"),
            linewidth = 1, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    title = "Rolling 1-Step-Ahead VAR Forecast vs Real Spread",
    y = "Spread (bps)",
    x = "Date",
    color = "Legend"
  ) +
  theme_bw()

# 7. Metriche sui livelli
err_level <- df_roll$RealSpread - df_roll$ForecastSpread

mae_level <- mean(abs(err_level), na.rm = TRUE)
rmse_level <- sqrt(mean(err_level^2, na.rm = TRUE))

cat("MAE levels:", round(mae_level, 4), "\n")
cat("RMSE levels:", round(rmse_level, 4), "\n")

# 8. Benchmark naive
naive_fc_level <- rep(last_level_before_test, length(fc_level))

mae_naive <- mean(abs(real_level - naive_fc_level), na.rm = TRUE)
rmse_naive <- sqrt(mean((real_level - naive_fc_level)^2, na.rm = TRUE))

cat("Naive MAE:", round(mae_naive, 4), "\n")
cat("Naive RMSE:", round(rmse_naive, 4), "\n")

# 9. Valore finale out-of-sample 1-step-ahead
final_forecast_date <- tail(test_dates, 1)
final_forecast_diff <- tail(fc_dspread, 1)
final_forecast_level <- tail(fc_level, 1)

cat("Final forecast date:", as.character(final_forecast_date), "\n")
cat("Final out-of-sample 1-step forecast (diff):", round(final_forecast_diff, 4), "\n")
cat("Final out-of-sample 1-step forecast (level):", round(final_forecast_level, 4), "\n")





```
Window ampia (es. 300): Il modello vede molta storia. I coefficienti sono più stabili perché mediati su un lungo periodo, ma il modello è "lento" a reagire a cambiamenti strutturali recenti (es. un improvviso aumento della volatilità o un cambio di politica monetaria).

Window piccola (es. 150): Il modello è molto reattivo. Si adatta rapidamente agli shock più recenti, ma i coefficienti sono più "nervosi" (instabili) perché basati su pochi dati. Questo può portare a previsioni molto diverse anche per piccoli cambiamenti nei dati.

SPiegare comportamento rol alto mae basso e viceversa
